PixiJS V5 Tutorial (35) - Polishing the Last Few Details

This is the last post in the series, so let's take our Treasure Hunter game and polish off a few rough edges — randomizing the monster speed on restart, making the player go semi-transparent on a hit, and dealing random collision damage instead of a fixed amount.
PixiJS V5 Tutorial (34) - Wiring Up the Collision Function to Win the Game

Time to make the treasure chest follow the explorer around. Once you grab it and walk to the door, the game shows a win. The good news is this part is way simpler than the collision math we built earlier — it's mostly just calling our collision function in the right places and tracking one little flag.
PixiJS V5 Tutorial (33) - Spawning a Swarm of Monsters

Time to build the monsters. Right now we've only got one slime, which isn't much of a threat — so let's spawn a whole bunch of them and make them move on their own. We'll loop to generate the slimes, push them into an array, update them in the game loop, and give each one a random speed so they don't all march in sync.
PixiJS V5 Tutorial (32) - Building a Restart Button From Scratch

A lighter one this time, picking up right where the last post left off. In this article we build a restart button for the Game Over screen — drawing the button with geometry, adding the label text, wiring up the click handler, and resetting everything back to its starting state.
PixiJS V5 Tutorial (31): Showing Game Over and Start Text Messages

Time to build the text message that pops up when the player dies from a collision. The trick here isn't the text itself — it's juggling two Containers, one shown and one hidden. This post walks through the death screen, the start screen, and how to flip between them with PixiJS.
JavaScript Core Concepts (30): Shallow Copy vs Deep Copy for Objects

Objects in JavaScript are passed by reference, which means copying them is trickier than it looks. In this post we'll dig into shallow copy vs deep copy — how Object.assign and the spread syntax both fall short, why the JSON.stringify trick works but has nasty edge cases, and what to reach for when you need a real deep clone.
JavaScript Objects: Call by Reference or Call by Sharing? (Core Concepts 29)

Is JavaScript Call by Reference or Call by Sharing? In this post we walk through evaluation strategies — Call by value, Call by reference, and Call by sharing — with simple examples, and figure out which one JavaScript actually uses when you pass objects around.
PixiJS V5 Tutorial (30) - The Game Loop

Time to build the game loop. In short, it all comes down to app.ticker.add((delta) => {...});. In this part we wire up an HP bar that ticks down over time, hook the collision detection into the loop, and black out the screen once HP hits zero.
JavaScript Core (28) - Objects - The Object Reference Exercise

This chapter is the exercise for object references. The whole point is to make sure the "objects are passed by reference" idea actually stuck. We walk through a tricky little snippet step by step, tracing memory pointers and drawing out exactly why a and b end up the way they do.
PixiJS V5 Tutorial (29) - Building treasureHunter (the Treasure Hunter)

Time to actually start building treasureHunter, our little Treasure Hunter game. In this part we'll slowly pull the pieces apart and put them back together to get a simple version up and running — loading the spritesheet, cutting out each sprite, and laying out the dungeon, monster, treasure, door, and player.
